pattern: Cutting the array into contiguous pieces and summing each piece turns the problem into choosing cut points so piece sums never decrease, and the most pieces you can get is found by tracking, per prefix, the best (piece count, smallest last-piece sum) pair, trying every earlier prefix as the start of the final piece.
hints: Think of the final array as the sums of contiguous pieces that partition the original array; you are choosing where to cut, and you want as many pieces as possible whose sums never decrease left to right.

For every prefix, remember the largest number of pieces it can be split into, and the smallest possible sum for the very last of those pieces -- extending forward means finding an earlier prefix whose remembered last-piece sum is small enough for the new piece (the rest of the array up to there) to beat or match.

For a prefix ending at position i, check every earlier cut point j: if the piece from j to i sums to at least j's remembered last-piece sum, that's a valid way to extend, so scan all of them and keep whichever gives the most pieces, breaking ties toward the smallest resulting sum.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, three elements collapse to one
statement example, one merge in the middle
two elements, largest possible drop forces one merge
all equal values need no merging
strictly decreasing run, tempting to assume only one piece survives
a large leading value forces several small trailing values to be merged together
a longer mixed sequence exercising many merge decisions at once

stress.description: random values at a large array length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: long strictly decreasing run forcing many merges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = [n - i for i in range(n)]
    return [nums]
